sentences.base_language: Early one Saturday morning, Seoyeon decided to go to the traditional market.
sentences.target_language: 어느 토요일 이른 아침, 서연은 재래시장에 가기로 결심했다.
sentences.romanized: Eoneu toyoil ireun achim, Seoyeon-eun jaeraesuijang-e gagi-ro gyeolsimhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**재래시장** (jaeraesuijang) = traditional market — **재래** (traditional/conventional) + **시장** (sijang, market). Distinct from **대형마트** (large mart/supermarket) and **편의점** (convenience store). The **재래시장** is the traditional Korean open-air or covered market with fresh food vendors, street food, and small shops — revived in recent years through local culture initiatives.
**-기로 결심하다** = to decide/resolve to do: **가기로** 결심했다 (resolved to go). **-기로** nominalizes the verb and marks the decision/plan. Compare: **-기로 하다** (to plan/decide — slightly softer, can be external agreement): 만나**기로 했다** (we planned to meet). **결심하다** (to resolve) is stronger and more personal than **결정하다** (to decide — can be more administrative/external).

sentences.base_language: She had always loved markets — the colors, the smells, the bustle.
sentences.target_language: 그녀는 언제나 시장을 좋아했다——그 색깔들, 냄새, 그리고 활기를.
sentences.romanized: Geunyeo-neun eonjena sijang-eul joahaeotda — geu saekkal-deul, naemsae, geurigo hwalgi-reul.
sentences.grammarNote:
**언제나** = always — interchangeable with **항상** (hangsang, always) in most contexts, but **언제나** has a slightly more literary or emotional tone: **언제나** 곁에 있다 (always by your side). Compare: **항상** (always — neutral, factual), **언제나** (always — often more heartfelt or poetic), **늘** (always — very colloquial): 늘 그랬어 (it's always been like that).
**활기** (hwalgi) = energy, vitality, liveliness — **활** (活, live/active) + **기** (氣, energy/spirit): the living energy of a busy place. **활기차다** (hwalgi-chada) = to be full of energy/bustling: 활기**찬** 시장 (a bustling market). This is the Korean equivalent of Chinese 热闹 and Japanese にぎわい — a positive, vivid quality. **활기가 넘치다** = to overflow with energy (literally).

sentences.base_language: She got dressed quickly and had a simple meal before leaving.
sentences.target_language: 빠르게 옷을 입고 간단히 아침을 먹은 뒤 집을 나섰다.
sentences.romanized: Ppareuge os-eul ipgo gandanhi achim-eul meogeun dwi jib-eul naseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-은/ㄴ 뒤** = after doing: 먹**은 뒤** (after eating). **-은/ㄴ 뒤** is a formal/written alternative to **-고 나서** (after doing — more spoken): 먹**고 나서** 나갔다 (went out after eating). Compare: **-은 다음에** (after — also common): 먹**은 다음에** 나갔다. All three mean 'after eating' — **-은 뒤** tends to be more formal/literary, **-고 나서** more conversational.
**나서다** = to step out, to set out (from home): 집을 **나섰다** (stepped out of/left home). **나서다** implies purposeful departure: 그가 앞장서서 **나섰다** (he took the lead and stepped forward). Compare: **나가다** (to go out — more general direction away from speaker): 방에서 **나갔다** (went out of the room). **집을 나서다** = to leave the house (set out on a journey or errand).

sentences.base_language: The market was about a fifteen-minute walk from her house.
sentences.target_language: 시장은 그녀의 집에서 걸어서 약 십오 분 거리에 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Sijang-eun geunyeo-ui jib-eseo georeoseo yak sibo bun geori-e isseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**걸어서** = on foot, by walking: **걷다** (to walk) → **걸어서** (by means of walking — using **-아/어서** to form an adverbial). **걸어서** + duration/distance: **걸어서** 오 분 (five minutes on foot). Compare modes of transport: **지하철로** (by subway — **로/으로** marks transport means), **버스로** (by bus), **걸어서** (on foot — note the different connective form). **걸어서** uses **-아/어서** while vehicles use **-(으)로**.
**약** (yak) = approximately — a formal/written word borrowed from Chinese (約): **약** 십오 분 (approximately fifteen minutes), **약** 삼십 명 (approximately thirty people). Compare: **대략** (daerak, roughly — slightly more colloquial), **거의** (geoui, almost/nearly — implies approach but not quite reaching): **약** is the most common formal approximation adverb in Korean.

sentences.base_language: When she arrived, the market was already packed with energy and noise.
sentences.target_language: 도착했을 때, 시장은 이미 활기와 소음으로 가득 차 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Dochakaesseul ttae, sijang-eun imi hwalgi wa soeum-euro gadeuk cha isseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**가득 차 있다** = to be full (resultant state): 가득 **차 있었다** (was full — the state of having been filled). **차다** (to become full) + **있다** (resultant state): **가득 차 있다** (is completely full). This **-아/어 있다** construction expresses a state resulting from a change: 문이 열려 **있다** (the door is open), 자리가 **차 있다** (the seat is taken/occupied).
**이미** = already — **이미** implies something has happened before the expected or reference time. Compare: **벌써** (already — often with a sense of surprise that it happened sooner than expected): 벌써 왔어요? (you're here already?). **이미** is more neutral: **이미** 끝났다 (it's already over — factual). **이미** is often used with past events as background information; **벌써** expresses surprise at the earliness.

sentences.base_language: A fishmonger was shouting out prices in a loud voice.
sentences.target_language: 한 생선 장수가 큰 목소리로 가격을 외치고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Han saengseon jangsuga keun moksori-ro gagyeok-eul oechigo isseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**장수** (jansu) = vendor, trader, peddler — a traditional word for a traveling or market seller: **생선** 장수 (fish vendor), **약** 장수 (medicine peddler), **엿** 장수 (taffy seller). **장수** is more traditional/colloquial than **판매원** (sales person) or **상인** (merchant). It has a warm, old-fashioned market feel — the kind of vendor who calls out prices in the **재래시장**.
**-(으)로** for means/manner: 큰 목소리**로** (with a loud voice — by means of a loud voice). **-(으)로** marks the means or instrument: 연필**로** 쓰다 (write with a pencil), 한국어**로** 말하다 (speak in Korean). This is the same **-(으)로** as for transport direction (서울**로** 가다, go to Seoul) and method — context clarifies the use.

sentences.base_language: Seoyeon asked a kimchi vendor: "How much is the kimchi?"
sentences.target_language: 서연은 김치 파는 곳에서 물었다. 「김치는 얼마예요?」
sentences.romanized: Seoyeon-eun gimchi paneun gos-eseo mureotda. 「Gimchi-neun eolmayeyo?」
sentences.grammarNote:
**파는 곳** = a place that sells / a vendor's stall: **팔다** (to sell) → **파는** (present tense modifier) + **곳** (place). A very natural way to refer to a vendor's location without naming it specifically: 채소 **파는 곳** (the vegetable stall), 빵 **파는 곳** (where they sell bread). Compare: **가게** (shop — a proper shop), **판매대** (sales counter — more formal), **파는 곳** (casual, everyday).
**얼마예요?** = how much is it?: **얼마** (how much) + **예요/이에요** (polite copula). The standard price inquiry. Compare: **얼마입니까?** (formal/deferential), **얼마예요?** (polite everyday), **얼마야?** (casual, close friends). **얼마나** = how much/how (for degrees): 얼마**나** 멀어요? (how far is it?). **얼마** alone is for prices and quantities.

sentences.base_language: The vendor answered: "One jar is ten thousand won. It's very well fermented."
sentences.target_language: 판매자가 대답했다. 「한 통에 만 원이에요. 잘 익었어요.」
sentences.romanized: Panmaeja-ga daedaphaeotda. 「Han tong-e man won-ieyo. Jal ikkeoseoyo.」
sentences.grammarNote:
**에** for price per unit: 한 통**에** 만 원 (ten thousand won per jar). The particle **에** is used to express price per unit or rate: 한 개**에** 오천 원 (5,000 won each), 하루**에** 한 번 (once per day). This **에** of ratio/rate is the same **에** that marks time expressions: 일주일**에** 세 번 (three times per week).
**익다** (ikda) = to ripen, to ferment, to be well-aged: 잘 **익은** 김치 (well-fermented kimchi). **익다** is used for natural processes of ripening or fermentation: 과일이 **익다** (fruit ripens), 된장이 **익다** (doenjang ferments). The past form **익었다** (has ripened/fermented) describes the completed state. **잘 익은** = well-fermented/properly ripened — a quality compliment for fermented foods like kimchi.

sentences.base_language: She bought a jar of kimchi, some bean sprouts, and fresh tofu.
sentences.target_language: 그녀는 김치 한 통, 콩나물 한 봉지, 그리고 두부를 샀다.
sentences.romanized: Geunyeo-neun gimchi han tong, kongnamul han bongji, geurigo dubu-reul satda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**통** (tong) = container, jar, barrel — a measure word for things in containers: **한 통** (one jar/container), 페인트 **두 통** (two cans of paint), 김치 **한 통** (one jar/pot of kimchi). **봉지** (bongji) = a bag/packet: 비닐 **봉지** (plastic bag), 콩나물 **한 봉지** (one bag of bean sprouts). Korean measure words must be memorized per item type: **통** (containers), **봉지** (bags/packets), **모** (tofu — 두부 **한 모**).
**콩나물** (kongnamul) = bean sprouts — **콩** (kong, soybean) + **나물** (namul, seasoned vegetable dish/greens). **나물** is both a specific type of preparation (seasoned greens) and a general word for edible plants. **콩나물** are soybean sprouts, a staple in Korean cooking: 콩나물국 (bean sprout soup), 콩나물무침 (seasoned bean sprouts). Distinct from **숙주** (sukju, mung bean sprouts — lighter and shorter).

sentences.base_language: At a fruit stand, she tried a slice of pear and found it incredibly juicy.
sentences.target_language: 과일 가게에서 배를 한 조각 맛봤는데, 믿을 수 없을 만큼 즙이 많았다.
sentences.romanized: Gwail gage-eseo bae-reul han jogak matswatnneunde, mideul su eopseul mankeum jeub-i manhatda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-았/었는데** = past tense with **-는데** (providing background or contrast): 맛봤**는데** 즙이 많았다 (she tasted it, and it had a lot of juice). **-는데** after a verb in past tense creates a pivot — it introduces what followed or contrasts: 먹었**는데** 정말 맛있었어 (I ate it, and it was really delicious). The **-는데** ending is one of the most versatile in Korean, linking clauses with nuances of background, contrast, or soft transition.
**믿을 수 없을 만큼** = to an unbelievable extent, incredibly: **믿다** (believe) + **-(으)ㄹ 수 없다** (cannot) + **만큼** (to the extent of). **만큼** of extent: 죽을 **만큼** 먹었다 (ate to the point of dying — ate so much), 울 **만큼** 힘들었다 (was hard enough to make you cry). **즙이 많다** = to be very juicy — **즙** (jeup, juice/sap) + **이 많다** (to have a lot of). A natural food description: 이 복숭아는 즙이 많아 (this peach is very juicy).

sentences.base_language: The vendor gave her an extra piece for free.
sentences.target_language: 판매자가 서비스로 한 조각 더 주었다.
sentences.romanized: Panmaeja-ga seobiseu-ro han jogak deo jueotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**서비스로** = as a service/bonus, for free as a gesture — **서비스** (seobiseu, from English 'service') in Korean market/restaurant culture specifically means an extra item given for free as a gesture of goodwill: **서비스**로 드릴게요 (I'll give you this for free). This use of 서비스 is a Koreanized meaning not found in standard English — a very common expression in Korean daily commerce.
**더** = more, additionally: **한 조각 더** (one more piece). **더** placed after a quantity or before a verb means 'more/additional': 하나 **더** 주세요 (please give me one more), 조금 **더** 기다려요 (wait a little more). Compare: **또** (moreover/again — adds another instance): **또** 왔어요? (you came again?). **더** expresses degree or quantity; **또** expresses repetition.

sentences.base_language: Her bags were getting heavier and her arms were starting to ache.
sentences.target_language: 가방이 점점 무거워지고, 팔이 아파오기 시작했다.
sentences.romanized: Gabang-i jeomjeom mugeowoJigo, pal-i apao-gi sijakhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**점점** = gradually, increasingly — **점점** + change verb: **점점** 무거워지다 (get gradually heavier). Compare: **점차** (gradually — more formal/written), **서서히** (slowly and gradually — even more gradual), **점점** (colloquial and very common). **점점** is the most everyday word for gradual change in Korean.
**-아/어오다** = to come (approaching the present) + verb: **아파오다** (to start to ache and be getting painful — pain is coming toward the present moment). **-아/어오다** expresses a change or action that develops and approaches the current state: 날이 밝아**오다** (day is getting bright), 더워**오다** (it's getting hot). Compare: **-아/어가다** (moving away into the future): 점점 나아**가다** (gradually getting better — going into the future). **오다** = toward; **가다** = away.

sentences.base_language: She suddenly ran into an old friend who was browsing the stalls.
sentences.target_language: 물건을 구경하고 있는 옛 친구를 우연히 마주쳤다.
sentences.romanized: Mulgeon-eul gugyeonghago inneun yet chingu-reul uyeonhi majucheotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**마주치다** = to run into, to come face to face with (accidentally): **우연히** 마주쳤다 (ran into by chance). **마주** (facing each other) + **치다** (to hit/strike): to bump face-to-face. A vivid and common verb for unexpected encounters. Compare: **만나다** (to meet — can be planned), **마주치다** (to run into — specifically face-to-face and unplanned), **우연히 만나다** (to meet by coincidence).
**구경하다** = to look around, to browse, to watch (as a spectator): 물건을 **구경하다** (browse goods/window-shop), 경치를 **구경하다** (take in the scenery). **구경** has a slightly leisurely, observational nuance — looking for the pleasure of seeing, not necessarily with intent to buy. Compare: **쇼핑하다** (to go shopping — more purposeful), **구경하다** (to browse/look around). **-고 있는** modifying a noun = the person who is currently doing: 자고 있는 아기 (a sleeping baby), 구경하고 있는 사람 (a person who is browsing).

sentences.base_language: They chatted for a while and made plans to have a meal together soon.
sentences.target_language: 잠깐 이야기를 나누다가 가까운 시일 내에 같이 밥 한번 먹기로 약속했다.
sentences.romanized: Jamkkan iyagi-reul nanuda-ga gakka-un siil nae-e gachi bap hanbeon meokgi-ro yaksokhaeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-다가** = while doing / in the middle of doing (and then): 이야기를 나누**다가** (while they were talking / in the middle of their conversation). **-다가** implies that the second action interrupts or follows from the first while it was in progress: 걷**다가** 넘어졌다 (fell while walking). Compare: **-면서** (while doing — two actions simultaneous throughout): 이야기하**면서** 걸었다 (walked while talking — both ongoing together). **-다가** implies transition; **-면서** implies true simultaneity.
**밥 한번 먹다** = to have a meal together (Korean cultural expression): **밥** (rice/meal) + **한번** (once) + **먹다** (eat). **밥 한번 먹자** (let's have a meal sometime) is a quintessentially Korean social expression — often a light promise to meet up. **한번** here doesn't mean exactly 'one time' but 'sometime/one of these days.' **가까운 시일 내에** (within the near future) is a more formal expression for 'soon.'

sentences.base_language: She bought some freshly made rice cakes at a traditional food stall.
sentences.target_language: 전통 먹거리 가게에서 갓 만든 떡을 조금 샀다.
sentences.romanized: Jeontong meokgeori gage-eseo gat mandeun tteok-eul jogeum satda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**갓** = just (very recently): **갓 만든** (just made), **갓 나온** (just released/come out), **갓 구운** (just baked). **갓** is an adverb placed before a modifier verb to indicate something was very recently done: **갓** 결혼한 부부 (a newlywed couple — just married). Compare: **방금** (just now — for a single recent moment): **방금** 전화했어요 (just called). **갓** is used specifically with modifiers before nouns; **방금** with main verbs.
**떡** (tteok) = Korean rice cake — a central element of Korean food culture, made from glutinous or non-glutinous rice flour. **떡** appears in countless varieties: **가래떡** (cylinder rice cake), **인절미** (pounded rice cake with bean powder), **떡볶이** (spicy stir-fried rice cake). **먹거리** (meokgeori) = food items, edibles — **먹다** (eat) + **거리** (material/stuff): things to eat. A colloquial term for food stalls or food as a category.

sentences.base_language: The smell of freshly cooked food made her stomach growl.
sentences.target_language: 갓 조리된 음식 냄새에 그녀의 배가 꼬르륵 울렸다.
sentences.romanized: Gat joridoen eumsik naemsae-e geunyeo-ui bae-ga kkoreuryeuk ullyeotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**꼬르륵** = the sound of a stomach growling — Korean onomatopoeia for stomach rumbling. Korean has rich sound-symbolic vocabulary: **보글보글** (bubbling of soup), **아삭아삭** (crunching of vegetables), **촉촉하다** (moist/dewy feel), **꼬르륵** (stomach growling). **울리다** = to ring, to resound, to make a sound — a causative/intransitive: 전화가 울리다 (the phone rings), 배가 **울리다** (the stomach rumbles). Compare: **울다** (to cry/weep — only for tears); **울리다** is for sounds.
**냄새에** = at the smell of, triggered by the smell: **냄새에** 배가 울렸다 (the stomach rumbled at the smell). The particle **에** here functions as a trigger or stimulus: 그 말**에** 웃었다 (laughed at those words), 소음**에** 잠이 깼다 (woke up at the noise). This **에** expresses what caused or triggered a reaction — a very common pattern for sensory or emotional responses.

sentences.base_language: She also found a stall selling handmade crafts and stopped to look.
sentences.target_language: 손으로 만든 공예품을 파는 곳도 발견하고 한참 들여다봤다.
sentences.romanized: Son-euro mandeun gongyepum-eul paneun got-do balgyeonhago hancham deullyeodabwatda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**들여다보다** = to look closely at, to peer into: 가게 안을 **들여다보다** (peer into the shop). **들여다** (inward look) + **보다** (look): the idea of looking carefully into or at something. Compare: **바라보다** (to gaze at/look at — from a distance), **들여다보다** (to peer closely into), **살펴보다** (to look around/examine carefully). Each captures a different quality of looking.
**한참** = for a good while, for a considerable time: **한참** 기다렸다 (waited for quite a while), **한참** 들여다봤다 (looked for a long time). **한참** implies a period that was longer than brief but shorter than very long — it has a sense of 'absorbed in the activity for a while.' Compare: **잠깐** (briefly, for a short time — short duration) vs **한참** (for a good while — longer, absorbed). **한참 동안** adds 동안 for emphasis: **한참 동안** 생각했다 (thought for quite a while).

sentences.base_language: By midday, her back ached and her feet were tired from all the walking.
sentences.target_language: 낮이 될 무렵에는 허리가 아프고 발도 많이 지쳐 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Naj-i doel mureop-e-neun heori-ga apeugo bal-do mani jichy-eo isseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄹ 무렵에** = around the time when ~ will happen: 낮이 될 **무렵에** (around the time noon was approaching). **무렵** = approximate time: **무렵** is used with modifying clauses to indicate an approximate time: 해질 **무렵** (around sunset), 도착할 **무렵** (around the time of arrival). Compare: **-을 때** (when — more precise): 낮이 됐**을 때** (when it became noon — exact point). **무렵** implies approximation.
**허리** (heori) = lower back/waist — in Korean, **허리** specifically refers to the lower back and waist area: **허리가 아프다** (lower back hurts), **허리를 펴다** (straighten one's back). Compare: **등** (deung, upper back/back in general). This distinction is important for describing physical symptoms accurately: **등이 아프다** (upper back hurts) vs **허리가 아프다** (lower back aches). **지쳐 있다** = to be exhausted (resultant state): **지치다** → **지쳐 있다** (exhausted).

sentences.base_language: She sat down at a small cafe and ordered a hot barley tea.
sentences.target_language: 시장 한편의 작은 카페에 앉아 따뜻한 보리차 한 잔을 주문했다.
sentences.romanized: Sijang hanpyeon-ui jag-eun kape-e anja ttatteuthan borija han jan-eul jumunhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**한편** (hanpyeon) = one side, a corner/part of: 시장 **한편** (one side/corner of the market). **한** (one) + **편** (side/direction): 길 **한편** (one side of the road), 마음 **한편** (one corner of the heart — a literary use). **한편** is also a conjunction meaning 'on the other hand' or 'meanwhile.' Here it's the spatial meaning: a corner or side of the market.
**보리차** (borija) = barley tea — **보리** (bori, barley) + **차** (cha, tea). Barley tea (roasted barley steeped in hot water) is the quintessential Korean household beverage — often drunk instead of plain water. A culturally important detail for Korean language learning. **따뜻하다** (ttatteutada) = to be warm/comfortably hot (for drinks and temperature): **따뜻한** 차 (warm tea), **따뜻한** 사람 (a warm person — figurative). Compare: **뜨겁다** (tteugeopda, hot — too hot to drink/touch): 뜨겁게 데워진 차 (scalding hot tea).

sentences.base_language: She arrived home satisfied, bags full of fresh food and a heart full of happiness.
sentences.target_language: 신선한 식재료가 가득 담긴 가방을 들고 흐뭇한 마음으로 집에 돌아왔다.
sentences.romanized: Sinseonian sikjaeryo-ga gadeuk damgin gabang-eul deulgo heumuthan maeum-euro jib-e dorawatda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**흐뭇하다** (heumutada) = to feel warmly satisfied, to feel content and pleased — a nuanced emotion word: **흐뭇한** 미소 (a satisfied, warm smile), **흐뭇하게** 바라보다 (look on with warm satisfaction). This word captures a quiet, inward contentment — often felt when something has gone just as hoped. Compare: **만족스럽다** (satisfying — more cognitive satisfaction), **뿌듯하다** (proud and fulfilled — achievement-related), **흐뭇하다** (warm, quiet contentment — often after a pleasant experience or seeing someone dear).
**담기다** = to be put in, to be placed in (passive of 담다 — to fill/put in): 식재료가 **담긴** 가방 (a bag that has food in it — the bag with food packed in it). **-ㄴ/은** modifier: **담긴** modifies 가방 (the bag that is packed). Compare: 내가 담은 가방 (the bag I packed — active) vs 식재료가 **담긴** 가방 (the bag in which food is packed — passive). Korean passive **-이/히/리/기-** or the **-아/어지다** form: **담기다** is the passive of **담다**.
